title: Sensation vs. Perception: From Energy to Meaning
caption: Physical energy becomes neural activity through transduction, but perception requires the nervous system to organize and interpret that signal.
alt_text: Flowchart showing physical stimulus, receptor transduction, neural signal, brain interpretation, and meaningful perception, with top-down expectations feeding back into interpretation.
attribution: Original diagram by Jon Oxford with AI assistance. No external assets.

title: Signal Detection Theory Matrix
caption: Signal detection theory separates what is actually present from what the observer reports, producing hits, misses, false alarms, and correct rejections.
alt_text: Two-by-two matrix crossing stimulus present versus absent with observer says yes versus no, labeling hit, miss, false alarm, and correct rejection.
attribution: Original diagram by Jon Oxford with AI assistance. No external assets.

title: The Retina Is Not a Camera Sensor
caption: Rods, cones, the fovea, and the blind spot show why visual sensation is already biologically organized before perception begins.
alt_text: Labeled eye cross-section showing light entering the pupil, focusing on the retina, with cones concentrated at the fovea, rods more common in the periphery, and a blind spot where the optic nerve exits.
attribution: Original diagram by Jon Oxford with AI assistance. No external assets.

title: Gestalt Principles in One Page
caption: Gestalt principles show how the visual system groups separate elements into organized wholes.
alt_text: Four panels illustrating figure-ground, proximity, similarity, and closure using simple dots, shapes, and incomplete outlines.
attribution: Original diagram by Jon Oxford with AI assistance. No external assets.

title: Cochlea: Place and Frequency Coding
caption: Place theory and frequency theory solve different parts of the pitch perception problem.
alt_text: Uncoiled basilar membrane labeled with high-frequency peaks near the base and low-frequency coding toward the apex, with an inset showing firing rate coding for low frequencies.
attribution: Original diagram by Jon Oxford with AI assistance. No external assets.

title: Gate Control Theory of Pain
caption: Pain is modulated at the spinal cord by incoming touch signals and descending signals from the brain.
alt_text: Diagram showing injury signals and touch signals entering the spinal cord, a modulatable gate, upward pain transmission to the brain, and descending brain signals that can partly close the gate.
attribution: Original diagram by Jon Oxford with AI assistance, based on Melzack and Wall’s gate control theory. No external assets.
